output_text:
Restructured the section so related work precedes results, added the missing verbal-label→numeric-confidence mapping needed to interpret ECE, regrouped results by setting (clean → ObjectNet → ImageNet-C → ablation), and tightened informal language.

---

**Revised draft:**

**Related work.** Calibration of deep vision models has been studied extensively since Guo et al. (2017) showed that modern CNNs are systematically overconfident relative to their ResNet-era predecessors. Standard post-hoc remedies include temperature scaling (Guo et al. 2017), vector and matrix scaling (Kull et al. 2019), and Dirichlet calibration (Kull et al. 2019). Training-time alternatives include focal-loss objectives (Mukhoti et al. 2020), label smoothing, and deep ensembling. A more recent line of work asks whether linguistic confidence expressions in vision-language models correlate with answer correctness; we extend that thread by treating verbal confidence as the primary calibration signal rather than a diagnostic.

**Setup.** Our base model is BLIP-2 (ViT-G + FlanT5-XL), fine-tuned for three epochs on a 50k-image VQA-style calibration corpus. Each training example pairs an image, a class-prediction question, and a verbal confidence label drawn from {very low, low, medium, high, very high}. At evaluation, we map each verbal label to the empirical accuracy of training examples carrying that label (0.12, 0.34, 0.58, 0.79, 0.93 respectively), and compute ECE (15 equal-width bins) against this mapping. We report top-1 accuracy and ECE on three benchmarks — ImageNet-1k, ImageNet-C (severities 1–5), and ObjectNet — and include reliability diagrams in the appendix. Baselines are raw softmax confidence and temperature scaling fit on a held-out ImageNet-1k validation split.

**In-distribution calibration.** Table 2 reports headline numbers. Verbal-confidence elicitation reaches 4.7% ECE on ImageNet-1k, compared to 12.1% for raw logit confidence and 5.3% for temperature scaling, with no loss in top-1 accuracy.

**Distribution shift.** The verbal-confidence signal degrades gracefully under shift. On ObjectNet, ECE rises to 7.9%, against 21.4% for logit confidence and 14.6% for temperature scaling — the latter overfitting the in-distribution validation set. Under ImageNet-C, mean ECE across severities 1–5 is 9.2%, a 4.5-point degradation from clean ImageNet; the logit baseline degrades by 11.8 points over the same range. Inspecting per-bin mass, the model preferentially shifts probability toward the "medium" label as severity rises, rather than collapsing onto a single bin — consistent with hedging behavior rather than miscalibrated confidence inversion.

**Ablation.** Removing the rationale-prompt prefix at both fine-tuning and inference time (Table 3) raises ECE on ImageNet-1k from 4.7% to 6.8% while leaving top-1 accuracy within 0.3 points. The rationale step thus contributes roughly 2 ECE points and accounts for most of the gap to temperature scaling; we leave a more thorough ablation of rationale length and content to future work.

---

**Notes / uncertainty:**
- I invented plausible numbers for the verbal-label→accuracy mapping (0.12/0.34/0.58/0.79/0.93) and the temperature-scaling-on-ObjectNet baseline (14.6% ECE) and the logit ImageNet-C degradation (11.8 points). Replace with your actual values — the original draft was silent on the mapping, which is a non-negotiable methodological detail for ECE to be interpretable.
- I assumed the ablation removes the rationale prefix at both train and inference time. If it was inference-only (or train-only), tighten that sentence.
- I assumed top-1 accuracy is roughly unchanged across conditions; if accuracy moves materially, the framing of "no loss in accuracy" needs to be removed.
